speaker: interviewer
text: This interview with Dr. Roden. Dr. Roden, you were just telling me something about the hospital that you worked in, in Auschwitz. Can you walk us into the hospital, and give us a feeling for what you saw and heard and even smelled?

speaker: interviewee
text: To call it hospital would be a little exaggeration. I would call it maximally hospice. It was the same block as the others-- the only thing is the front was converted. In the front, there was a-- the bedroom of the head of the hospital compound and the block. And on the other side, there was some kind of a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] space, which could be an office with a table-- office table. And then, attached to it was a room where there was an operating table-- something like an operating room. Across from it was a small room, which was a magazine for supplies-- mostly the bread which was brought in, was kept for about two or three days. And then there was a very primitive type of pharmacy, where two prison pharmacists were preparing whatever-- I mean, we brought sometimes from the incoming transport, so that sometimes we had some medicaments, which were left to us. Also, they were preparing special stuff. A tablet was put together, simply a kind of a container out of edible type of material, and the powder was put into it-- as more primitive kind of capsules were done that way. What was the medication which was to our disposal was very primitive. Sometimes we got sulfa drugs, which was unusual. And we had aspirin, or acetaminophen. And that would be about the extent of it-- or exceptional when they wanted or brought something unusual, like when they probably spread some kind of a disease through the camp. For sometimes, part of the block-- one has to imagine that you have got about, on each side, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 15 rows of triple bunks, on which there were sick persons, the sick prisoners lying. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what one could do for them was extremely limited. We had some material for pain, occasionally. So that was distributed. It was even, when we brought enough supply, even injected. As far as the surgery which was done there, which I witnessed, was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] couple of times there was an emergency appendectomy performed. Then there was performed a sterilization on one of the females. I'm sure it was under the sterilization program, which was done by Mengele. Then, I have seen probatory kind of a lobotomy-- cut into the abdomen of a couple of twins, female twins. Whether samples were taken, or whether it was observed or inspected by Mengele, himself, I cannot report.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me more about these surgeries that you witnessed? Under what conditions were they performed?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were performed under ether, under ether. And I don't think we had any local anesthetics-- no, ether, or chloroform. And I was present about on three, four occasions when abortions were done on prisoner women-- women, for to be pregnant was a certain death. No pregnancy allowed, naturally, no possible showing of being pregnant. So it was a kind of merciful act which had to be done very secretly, without the knowledge of the SS. There were three prisoner doctors who were very humane, and who I have never seen doing anything wrong. There were about 16 or 20 nursing personnel, who probably, when they found out a corpse, so obviously they confiscated the bread or whatever supply there was, which was quite understandable. I can give you the name-- names of the doctors. There was Dr. Bardach, B-A-R-D-A-C-H, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Dr. Heller, H-E-L-L-E-R, and Dr. Winternitz, W-I-- W-I-N-T-E-R-N-I-T-Z. Two of them went with me then from Auschwitz to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Schwarzheide. And one of them survived, Bardach survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what made these doctors different from the others?

speaker: interviewee
text: There had been many doctors in the camp, and these were chosen and were selected, I think, by the SS doctors. Besides Mengele, I remember only Klein.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they different for the way they treated the patients? How did you-- how were they distinguishable?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were no other doctors there, so they were distinguishable-- no, they stayed human beings. You know, under the circumstances you worked, then under circumstances where it was done, it-- sometimes it was very difficult to stay that decent human being.

speaker: interviewer
text: And other staff in the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had all kinds of orderlies and nurses-- there was a nursing personnel, as I mentioned. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then there have been-- there haven't been any kapos, any overseers there. That was a-- that was a-- that was, I mean, a lucky stroke for this. They were usually in attendance to show themselves off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the patients ever ill treated?

speaker: interviewee
text: The patients, no, were not-- well, they were under-treated, naturally, for we didn't have the medication. But they have not-- nobody was beaten, nobody was, I mean, shot. Only at one, two occasions there was a selection. In other words, they took out people who were sick so that they would fill somewhere a quota-- which was missing the quota. It's a kind of tragic to mention it, under the name of quota. The plan, the general plan, in Birkenau was to gas and cremate 10,000 people a day, which is a harrowing task. Actually, the gassing was simpler than the burning of the bodies. The crematoria took only a certain amount of the bodies. So they had to open-- they had to dig open ditches, and staple the body, and burn them. That was this smell, tremendous smell, which was permeating all through the compound. Also, when you were getting up in the morning, so you had to dust such an orange dust off the blanket, if you were lucky enough to have a blanket, or off your clothing. Now, when they were short of this quota, which they, by the way, increased in 1944, when the Hungarian Jews were coming. For about two months, they increased it to 20,000 a day. So they made a selection in the camps. And it was obvious to everybody what you are selected for. But we succeeded to let the people who really were sick sick be selected. I had one experience which was kind of really moving-- when we had been in one of our trips to Israel, so sitting there in the kibbutz and just talking, suddenly appear there two women on the scene. And I got a bear hug. And I was explained, I am looking for you for years. I know that you were coming here, but I couldn't find you. I says, I can't even place you. I don't know who you are. He says, I am so and so. And you saved my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Saved your life? How did I do it? So, what had happened, obviously, was that I have seen-- we have seen that the SS is coming for to select some of the patients. So I barged in. I said, kids, out, out, out from here, as quickly as you can! Don't ask any questions! Don't look for anything to put on, just run!

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was it. That was one of them. Race out of sight.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were patients in the hospital at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they were pretending he was something-- hiding from something. But it doesn't make any difference. Obviously, the position I was in brought in unlimited amount of food, which I distributed not only to he and to most parents, but I was able to feed, I don't know how many dozens of people. For every dead person, if one reported the dead-- the death after the appel-- we got the ration for the dead person, which could be given to other people, three other people. I got out-- outside of the camp, as I mentioned before, under circumstances which should be difficult to describe. For one, I had to bribe the SS men to keep-- leave us was alone, and mostly to be able to bring into the camp stuff, which certainly, would be seized and we would be severely punished, if not killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say out of the camp, you don't mean out of Auschwitz-Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. We were-- let's say we were sent to this camp called Kanada, to bring from there blankets or, I don't know, something ridiculous like blouse, or some supplies, medical supplies. And at the same time, we had to pick up a gold watch for the SS man who was at the gate, for he ordered, that he wanted a gold watch as a bribe. And we succeeded to bring in substantial amount of gasoline, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a rifle, couple of revolvers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was a underground-- mostly generated by the Russians, in the Russians camp, Russian prisoners of war, who were punished by being put then-- for escape, put to Auschwitz. And most were the communist underground. In a certain way, I was a member of this underground, for we didn't know each other. And my only contribution and duty was to bring something which could help us in case that we will start an uprising. The uprising simply would be performed in a way that we will use the few primitive weapons we had in different camps, and the gasoline, which will be used to put the wooden barracks aflame. And in the chaos, try to kill some of the SS men, get the weaponry, try to get a hold of some of these guards on the-- on the towers, watch towers. And try to cause a massive escape, if possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately when did you do this? When did you bring these things into the Czech camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was approximately between March and July 1944-- 40-- '44, yes. And also, at one of these trips in Kanada, I met one of the members, by the name of the Zoshek, was his first name. He belonged-- he was the kapo of the sonderkommando-- special commando which was the commando which handled the gas chambers and the crematoria. And I arranged with him that I was given a supply, a bag of potassium cyanide. The potassium cyanide, in our primitive pharmacy, we put into this edible paper-like [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] capsules. And I gave it to members of my family, and many friends. We were-- we were successful to make about 25 of these capsules out of the supply. Simply, our idea was that, should we be in a situation that we are being taken to the gas chambers-- so you just give them the pleasure of biting into the capsule, and that would be it. And I do sincerely hope that my father and my mother-in-law did so, when they went into the gas chambers. The uprising, [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] towards the-- at the beginning of June in 1944, they came SS men to the camp. And everybody went through a selection. Selection means simply that you marched in front of a group of SS men, usually drunk, extremely abusive. You marched on this brick kind of tunnel between the two chimneys. And they were selecting people who is capable to go work, and who isn't. With the males, it went relatively smoothly. And with the females, it was a living tragedy, for they were spitting. First of all, they were naked. The women were naked, and they were making fun of them, and screaming at them, and spitting at them. Anyhow, we were divided into people who were capable of work, and who were not. And then they made a transport of thousands men, young men. I was in that group. And we were supposed, on the 1st of July, 1944, to leave the camp to be transferred somewhere to a different camp for work. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So we gathered, and under command of the SS men, about 50 SS men with some machine guns. And they marched out of the gate-- which was wide open for us, five abreast. And they took us to the one road which went through the camp number two, and camp number three, which was built. On the right side were two crematoria, hidden in these birches. On the left side, there was something called sauna. The sauna was actually where-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] showers with all kinds of facilities and similar. And there was also the distribution of clothing, of prisoner clothing. And as we marched, they let us halt at the point where there was the crossover between the crematoria and between the sauna. We knew already that the special commando was not called to do duty. We knew that there is a train, empty train, with a normal, I mean, personal wagon standing there. And we didn't know whether this is all a game or not. And if we would get the order to go right, that would be the call for the uprising. After standing there for 20 minutes, we got the order-- turn left. And we went with a tremendous speed through the sauna. No, they usually cut your hair, and they shaved you all over. Nothing, nothing of that-- just quickly. They threw at us various types of this striped clothing. And the size, we had to change everything ourselves, among ourselves, for you got-- I mean, the trousers which were up until here, the guy who was too tall-- tall got the small. But we managed. And they took us to this train. And we entered the train-- we were 40 only in one wagon, 40 persons. I mean, prisoner was never a person. And they were two soldiers in the wagon. And there was, I mean, a big kind of barrel with water. And there was a big drum for human facilities. And there was, I mean, some kind of a chemical to put into this. And there was whistling and yelling and God knows what. And the train pulled out. And we simply just looked, and the train was going into a turn. We looked out, and there was written [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And we were out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were trying to get out of these soldiers-- where are we going? What is it all about? And they were scared to talk, for they were given, I mean, all kinds of instructions, I'm sure. But they didn't touch us, didn't hear, didn't say one word.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they slowly, realizing that we talk German, if necessary. So they started to talk to us. And they were scared to-- to even to permit us to take our excreta out. They did it themselves. And when we were already in about 10 hours in the train-- so finally, we got out of them, we are going to do something which is [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a commando camp of the main camp of Sachsenhausen-Oranienburg, Sachsenhausen-- S-A-C-H-S-A-N-H-A-U-S-E-N hyphen capital O-R-I-E-N-B-U-R-G. This was the oldest-- there is Mauthausen or Buchenwald, is the oldest concentration camp created by Hitler in 1933 for political prisoners. And we were one of the side commandos. And the name of the place is Schwarzheide, written S-C-H-W-A-R-Z-H-E-I-D-E, translated, black meadow. Black meadow sounds odious, using the same word.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the conditions of the prisoners like on arrival on--at Schwarzheide?

speaker: interviewee
text: At Schwarzheide? They gave us food, they gave us bread. And they gave us something. I don't remember what it was-- but they gave us food on the way. And the soldiers didn't touch us. I mean, we came out on a platform, which was called Ruhland, R-U-H-L-A-N-D, the land of peace. And it really existed. Again, arranged by five. And we were marched by SS men, who simply didn't know how to handle us-- they were from the main camp. They were already seasoned troopers. And they marched us into a camp. I have got the plan of the camp here. The barracks-- they were barracks which were built for prisoners, obviously. And they had triple bunks. But there had been only very limited amount of bunks in each barrack, so that we were divided, let's say, in one of these things were not more that than 100 people or 150 people. It was not overcrowded.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Roden, before we get into a description of Schwarzheide, I want to return to Auschwitz for a few more moments. You had said that there was some communication between the camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you elaborate on that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes-- you know, we had a kind of an Indian signal system. It wasn't, I mean, a system that you used fire and smoke. But you used signs in a sign language, which was transferred from one camp to the other. And it was done only at certain hour on a different place, so that it couldn't be traced between different blocks. And one could do it that you walked, and you just turned around and you just did-- and it was a signal. And that signal, the system of the signals, changed. I know that it started with a kind of alphabet, which we had at school-- usually K, A, H, J, or similar. Then it was, I mean, a number. And then it was, I mean, better, worse. Then it was-- which became quite obvious and became a part of the kind of silent language between the camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you involved in officially signaling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, occasionally I did. You know, I knew only one person who was actually responsible for our camp. His name was Hugo Lengfeld-- H-U-G-O L-E-N-G-F-E-L-D, Lengfeld. And who returned with me, went with me all through the camps, with the death march, and who became then one of the Grey Eminence of the Communist party in Czechoslovakia, so that--

speaker: interviewer
text: And was this is the system that enabled you to understand that the sonderkommando had not been called up for duty?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, it was reported simply. Sonderkommando was very simple-- sonderkommando was this, this.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would have happened if your group had turned to the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: The shooting would start. We would probably attack our guards. They would start to burn the camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who had the weapons then? Who had the gasoline and the weapons?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody in the camp. But we would have to get the weapons ourselves. Probably we would be sacrificed, but still, it would be a sign that this is the start of the total destruction. Or, as you know, all the camps, the destruction of camps before they all have been annihilated-- whether it was Treblinka, whether it was Majdanek, whether it was Chelmno, whether it was Sobibor-- they all were annihilated. So we knew that this would be something of enormous dimension. But still, with the Germans, everything was feasible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall the day that you were on the wagon with the gasoline and the revolvers, bringing it back into the Czech camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember exactly the day, but it was-- it's very limited. It must have been either in April, May, June, 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you recall your feeling at that moment? You were breaking one of your rules.

speaker: interviewee
text: I still have something left from the camp, and that is, I have difficulty to be afraid. Sometimes it's good, sometimes fear is a-- could be a life-saving measure. Let's say, I am driving and I am in a skid. Everybody is just fainting. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I just get out of the skid. That's all. Or something happens, I mean-- absolute upset, tragedy in front of me. I am missing the fear. You were asking how I felt. Nothing. Nix. For I knew perfectly well if we are caught, we are corpses within a few minutes, within in a few minutes. But no, I cannot tell you that I was afraid the same way-- I didn't have a feeling of great achievement. I think, I considered it for some kind of my duty if I had the access.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was very good in bribing the SS. Dr. Roden, we're going to take a break right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.
